title: Antenatal Visit: Danger Signs Screening
short_description: Learn how to handle Antenatal Visit: Danger Signs Screening correctly based on WHO protocols.

questions.patient_prompt: You are conducting a routine ANC visit. Meena, 6 months pregnant, says she has been feeling fine. You check her blood pressure and find the diastolic reading is 85 mmHg.
questions.mcq_question: What is your next step according to protocol?

options.text: Immediately refer her to hospital — it is an emergency
Give her antihypertensive medication right away
Repeat the blood pressure reading after 1 hour of rest
Record it as normal and continue the visit

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! As per the ANC Pre-eclampsia Screening Protocol (Section C3): 'If diastolic blood pressure is 90 mmHg, repeat after 1 hour rest.' A single reading of 85 mmHg is not enough to classify — the reading must be repeated to confirm.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. As per the ANC Pre-eclampsia Screening Protocol (Section C3): 'If diastolic blood pressure is 90 mmHg, repeat after 1 hour rest.' A single reading of 85 mmHg is not enough to classify — the reading must be repeated to confirm.

questions.patient_prompt: During Sita's 7th-month ANC visit, you find her diastolic BP is 95 mmHg on two separate readings taken 1 hour apart. She also has 2+ protein in her urine. However, she reports no headache, no blurred vision, and no epigastric pain.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct classification?

options.text: Severe Pre-eclampsia — refer urgently with magnesium sulphate
Pre-eclampsia — refer to hospital
Normal Hypertension — advise rest and follow up in one week
Risk of Pre-eclampsia — give aspirin and calcium

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Per Section C3: Diastolic BP 90–110 mmHg on two readings AND 2+ proteinuria, WITHOUT severe symptoms (headache, blurred vision, epigastric pain) = Pre-eclampsia, not Severe Pre-eclampsia. Action: Revise birth plan and refer to hospital.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Per Section C3: Diastolic BP 90–110 mmHg on two readings AND 2+ proteinuria, WITHOUT severe symptoms (headache, blurred vision, epigastric pain) = Pre-eclampsia, not Severe Pre-eclampsia. Action: Revise birth plan and refer to hospital.

questions.patient_prompt: Kavita is 8 months pregnant. On ANC screening, her diastolic BP is 115 mmHg. She complains of severe headache and blurred vision. Urine shows 3+ protein. She has no prior history of hypertension.
questions.mcq_question: What is the MOST CRITICAL immediate action before referring her?

options.text: Give her paracetamol for headache and advise rest
Give oral antihypertensive tablets and observe for 30 minutes
Give magnesium sulphate and appropriate antihypertensives, then refer urgently
Start iron supplementation and refer at next available transport

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C3 + Emergency Treatment B13: Diastolic BP ≥110 mmHg with 3+ proteinuria AND severe symptoms = Severe Pre-eclampsia. Protocol mandates giving magnesium sulphate AND antihypertensives BEFORE urgent referral. Delay is life-threatening.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C3 + Emergency Treatment B13: Diastolic BP ≥110 mmHg with 3+ proteinuria AND severe symptoms = Severe Pre-eclampsia. Protocol mandates giving magnesium sulphate AND antihypertensives BEFORE urgent referral. Delay is life-threatening.

questions.patient_prompt: During ANC, Rekha (28 weeks pregnant) has no danger symptoms but mentions she had pre-eclampsia in a previous pregnancy. Her current BP is 80 mmHg diastolic, no proteinuria, and no other signs. She lives in a low calcium-intake area.
questions.mcq_question: Which TWO preventive interventions should you provide according to WHO protocol?

options.text: Magnesium sulphate and bed rest
Iron folate only — no other medication is needed yet
Low-dose aspirin AND calcium supplementation
Antihypertensive drug and hospital referral

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C3 — Risk of Pre-eclampsia: Women with prior pre-eclampsia history classify as 'Risk of Pre-eclampsia'. Protocol states: 'Give aspirin' AND 'Give calcium if low dietary intake area.' Both are mandatory preventive measures in this context.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C3 — Risk of Pre-eclampsia: Women with prior pre-eclampsia history classify as 'Risk of Pre-eclampsia'. Protocol states: 'Give aspirin' AND 'Give calcium if low dietary intake area.' Both are mandatory preventive measures in this context.

questions.patient_prompt: Pooja is 32 weeks pregnant with severe headache, blurred vision, and epigastric pain. Diastolic BP is 112 mmHg. She received magnesium sulphate loading dose IV 20 minutes ago. Before the next dose, you check: respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, knee jerk absent, urine output 80 ml in last 4 hours.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct decision regarding the next dose of magnesium sulphate?

options.text: Give the next dose — 20 minutes have passed since the loading dose
Reduce the dose to half and administer IM
Do NOT give the next dose — three contraindication signs are present
Switch to diazepam and continue with the next scheduled magnesium sulphate dose later

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B13: 'DO NOT give the next dose if ANY of these signs: knee jerk absent / urine output <100 ml/4 hrs / respiratory rate <16/min.' All three contraindications are present. Antidote if toxicity: calcium gluconate 1g IV.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B13: 'DO NOT give the next dose if ANY of these signs: knee jerk absent / urine output <100 ml/4 hrs / respiratory rate <16/min.' All three contraindications are present. Antidote if toxicity: calcium gluconate 1g IV.

title: Antenatal Anaemia Screening
short_description: Learn how to handle Antenatal Anaemia Screening correctly based on WHO protocols.

questions.patient_prompt: You are screening Lakshmi, who is 5 months pregnant, for anaemia. She looks pale. You check her haemoglobin — it is 9 g/dl. She is not short of breath and has no other complaints.
questions.mcq_question: How should you classify and treat her?

options.text: Severe Anaemia — refer urgently to hospital
Moderate Anaemia — give double dose of iron tablet twice daily for 3 months
Normal — give standard single iron tablet once daily
No treatment needed — pallor alone is not a sign of anaemia

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C4 — Check for Anaemia: Haemoglobin 7–11 g/dl = Moderate Anaemia. Treatment: double dose of iron (1 tablet twice daily for 3 months), counsel on compliance, and reassess at next ANC visit in 4–6 weeks.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C4 — Check for Anaemia: Haemoglobin 7–11 g/dl = Moderate Anaemia. Treatment: double dose of iron (1 tablet twice daily for 3 months), counsel on compliance, and reassess at next ANC visit in 4–6 weeks.

questions.patient_prompt: During an ANC visit, you observe that Sunita (7 months pregnant) has severe palmar and conjunctival pallor. You are in a rural setting with no haemoglobin testing equipment available. Her breathing rate is 32 breaths per minute.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct classification and action?

options.text: Moderate Anaemia — give iron and reassess in 4 weeks
Normal variation — pallor in pregnancy is common, advise nutrition
Severe Anaemia — revise birth plan for facility with blood transfusion services and refer urgently
Risk of Anaemia — give iron folate and monitor weekly

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C4: Severe palmar AND conjunctival pallor WITH ≥30 breaths/min = Severe Anaemia, even without Hb measurement. Protocol: Revise birth plan for facility with blood transfusion services, give double dose iron, refer urgently to hospital.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C4: Severe palmar AND conjunctival pallor WITH ≥30 breaths/min = Severe Anaemia, even without Hb measurement. Protocol: Revise birth plan for facility with blood transfusion services, give double dose iron, refer urgently to hospital.

questions.patient_prompt: Priya is at her first ANC visit at 12 weeks. Her Hb is 11.5 g/dl. There is no pallor. She has no complaints.
questions.mcq_question: What iron supplementation should she receive?

options.text: No supplementation needed — Hb is within normal range
Double dose iron tablet twice daily — as a precaution in pregnancy
Iron tablet once daily for 3 months — standard preventive supplementation
IV iron infusion — oral iron is insufficient in pregnancy

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C4 — No Clinical Anaemia: Hb ≥11 g/dl with no pallor = No Clinical Anaemia. Protocol still mandates: 'Give iron 1 tablet once daily for 3 months' as routine preventive supplementation during pregnancy. Counsel on compliance.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C4 — No Clinical Anaemia: Hb ≥11 g/dl with no pallor = No Clinical Anaemia. Protocol still mandates: 'Give iron 1 tablet once daily for 3 months' as routine preventive supplementation during pregnancy. Counsel on compliance.

questions.patient_prompt: Geeta is 8 months pregnant and in early labour. You identify severe conjunctival pallor and her Hb is 6.5 g/dl. Breathing rate is 28/min.
questions.mcq_question: According to protocol, what is the correct approach given she is already in early labour?

options.text: Refer urgently to hospital immediately — do not wait for delivery
Proceed with delivery at primary care level, give double iron dose, monitor closely
Give blood transfusion at primary care facility and then allow normal delivery
Wait until delivery is complete before referring, to avoid risk during transport

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C4 + Labour Protocol: Severe Anaemia (Hb <7 g/dl) with pallor in early labour = refer urgently to hospital immediately. Only in late labour (imminent delivery) should delivery be completed before referral with intensive monitoring.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C4 + Labour Protocol: Severe Anaemia (Hb <7 g/dl) with pallor in early labour = refer urgently to hospital immediately. Only in late labour (imminent delivery) should delivery be completed before referral with intensive monitoring.

questions.patient_prompt: At 28 weeks follow-up, Radha's Hb is 8.5 g/dl (moderate anaemia). She received the first month of double-dose iron tablets but stopped after 2 weeks due to nausea. She believes her diet is sufficient.
questions.mcq_question: As an ASHA worker, what is the MOST IMPORTANT combined action?

options.text: Prescribe anti-nausea medication and double the iron dose again
Accept her decision and advise dietary improvement only
Counsel on compliance with the treatment, address side effects, reassess at next visit in 4–6 weeks
Classify her as non-compliant and refer directly to hospital

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section C4 — Moderate Anaemia Follow-up: Protocol emphasizes 'Counsel on compliance with treatment' as a mandatory step alongside giving double iron dose. Reassessment at next ANC visit (4–6 weeks) is required. If anaemia persists, refer to hospital.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section C4 — Moderate Anaemia Follow-up: Protocol emphasizes 'Counsel on compliance with treatment' as a mandatory step alongside giving double iron dose. Reassessment at next ANC visit (4–6 weeks) is required. If anaemia persists, refer to hospital.

title: Postnatal Follow-up: Heavy Bleeding (PPH)
short_description: Learn how to handle Postnatal Follow-up: Heavy Bleeding (PPH) correctly based on WHO protocols.

questions.patient_prompt: Sunita has just delivered her baby. The placenta has been delivered and is complete. You notice the uterus feels soft and is not well contracted. There is no heavy bleeding at this moment.
questions.mcq_question: What is the FIRST action you should take?

options.text: Immediately refer to hospital
Give ergometrine 0.2 mg IM right away
Massage the uterine fundus until it is hard and well contracted
Insert an IV line and wait to see if bleeding increases

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B10 — Bleeding: When uterus is soft/not well contracted, the FIRST step is: 'Place cupped palm on uterine fundus... Massage fundus in a circular motion until uterus is well contracted.' Uterine massage is the initial intervention before drugs or referral.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B10 — Bleeding: When uterus is soft/not well contracted, the FIRST step is: 'Place cupped palm on uterine fundus... Massage fundus in a circular motion until uterus is well contracted.' Uterine massage is the initial intervention before drugs or referral.

questions.patient_prompt: Meera delivered 30 minutes ago. The placenta is delivered and complete. Despite uterine massage, the uterus remains soft. Heavy bleeding continues — the pad soaks in under 5 minutes.
questions.mcq_question: According to WHO protocol, what is the correct sequence of immediate actions?

options.text: Give ergometrine 0.2 mg IV, apply pressure, refer
Give oxytocin 10 IU IM, insert IV line with 20 IU oxytocin at 60 drops/min, empty bladder, check BP every 15 min
Give misoprostol 4 tablets under the tongue first, then call for help
Apply aortic compression, give diazepam, and refer immediately

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B4 — Postpartum Heavy Bleeding: 'Call for extra help. Massage uterus and give oxytocin 10 IU IM. Insert an IV line and give IV fluids with 20 IU oxytocin at 60 drops/min. Empty bladder. Check and record BP and pulse every 15 minutes.'
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B4 — Postpartum Heavy Bleeding: 'Call for extra help. Massage uterus and give oxytocin 10 IU IM. Insert an IV line and give IV fluids with 20 IU oxytocin at 60 drops/min. Empty bladder. Check and record BP and pulse every 15 minutes.'

questions.patient_prompt: Anjali delivered 1 hour ago. Heavy bleeding continues despite uterine massage and oxytocin. IV oxytocin is unavailable at this rural health post.
questions.mcq_question: What is the WHO-recommended alternative?

options.text: Give ergometrine 0.5 mg IM immediately
Apply bimanual compression only and transport to hospital
Give misoprostol 4 tablets (800 mcg) sublingually
Give oral misoprostol 400 mcg and wait 30 minutes

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B10 + WHO PPH Recommendations: 'If IV oxytocin not available or if bleeding does not respond to oxytocin, give misoprostol — 4 tablets of 200 mcg (800 mcg) under the tongue.' This is the explicitly stated alternative. Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertensive patients.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B10 + WHO PPH Recommendations: 'If IV oxytocin not available or if bleeding does not respond to oxytocin, give misoprostol — 4 tablets of 200 mcg (800 mcg) under the tongue.' This is the explicitly stated alternative. Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertensive patients.

questions.patient_prompt: Padma delivered at home 45 minutes ago. The placenta has NOT been delivered yet. She is bleeding heavily. You arrive at the scene and the placenta remains undelivered 1 hour after the baby's birth.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct sequence of management according to protocol?

options.text: Wait another 30 minutes and attempt controlled cord traction again
Give ergometrine IM to contract the uterus and deliver the placenta
Attempt manual removal of placenta, give IV antibiotics, refer urgently with oxytocin IV infusion during transport
Give misoprostol under the tongue and massage uterus only

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B11 + B4: 'If placenta not delivered 1 hour after delivery, remove placenta manually. Give appropriate IM/IV antibiotics. If unable to remove, refer urgently. During transfer, continue IV fluids with 20 IU oxytocin at 30 drops/min.' Ergometrine is CONTRAINDICATED with retained placenta.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B11 + B4: 'If placenta not delivered 1 hour after delivery, remove placenta manually. Give appropriate IM/IV antibiotics. If unable to remove, refer urgently. During transfer, continue IV fluids with 20 IU oxytocin at 30 drops/min.' Ergometrine is CONTRAINDICATED with retained placenta.

questions.patient_prompt: Rani delivered vaginally 20 minutes ago. Bleeding has been controlled. Uterus is now hard and round. Blood loss was estimated at 280 ml. You are at a rural primary health post.
questions.mcq_question: According to protocol, what is the MINIMUM standard of care for the next few hours?

options.text: Discharge after 2 hours if no further bleeding occurs
Refer to hospital because blood loss exceeded 250 ml
Keep at facility for 24 hours, monitor every 30 minutes for 4 hours (BP, pulse, vaginal bleeding, uterus)
Give a second dose of oxytocin prophylactically and discharge after 1 hour

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B4 + D19-D20: For blood loss ≥250 ml that has stopped: 'Plan to keep the woman in the facility for 24 hours. Monitor intensively every 30 minutes for 4 hours — blood pressure, pulse, vaginal bleeding, uterus.' WHO states: 'DO NOT discharge before 24 hours' after uncomplicated vaginal birth.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B4 + D19-D20: For blood loss ≥250 ml that has stopped: 'Plan to keep the woman in the facility for 24 hours. Monitor intensively every 30 minutes for 4 hours — blood pressure, pulse, vaginal bleeding, uterus.' WHO states: 'DO NOT discharge before 24 hours' after uncomplicated vaginal birth.

title: Labour: Danger Signs & Emergency Management
short_description: Learn how to handle Labour: Danger Signs & Emergency Management correctly based on WHO protocols.

questions.patient_prompt: Kamala is in active labour. You check the fetal heart rate and find it is 170 beats per minute. You reposition her to her left side. After 30 minutes of observation, FHR remains at 168 bpm.
questions.mcq_question: What do you do next?

options.text: Continue monitoring — this is normal in labour
Give the mother IV fluids and oxygen only
If early labour, refer urgently to hospital. If late labour, call for help, monitor after every contraction, prepare for newborn resuscitation
Give oxytocin to speed up delivery

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section D14 — Fetal Heart Rate Problems: FHR >160 bpm after 30 minutes of observation = Baby Not Well. Protocol: 'If early labour → refer urgently to hospital. If late labour → call for help, monitor after every contraction, prepare for newborn resuscitation.'
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section D14 — Fetal Heart Rate Problems: FHR >160 bpm after 30 minutes of observation = Baby Not Well. Protocol: 'If early labour → refer urgently to hospital. If late labour → call for help, monitor after every contraction, prepare for newborn resuscitation.'

questions.patient_prompt: Seema is in labour. You perform a vaginal examination and suddenly notice the umbilical cord is visible at the vulva. The fetal heart rate is detectable.
questions.mcq_question: What is the MOST critical immediate action?

options.text: Allow normal labour to continue — cord prolapse resolves naturally
Give oxytocin to speed up delivery immediately
Manage as prolapsed cord — this is an emergency requiring urgent action and immediate referral
Perform a vaginal delivery at the health post with available assistance

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section D14: 'Cord seen at vulva = PROLAPSED CORD — Manage urgently.' This is an obstetric emergency. The protocol flags this as a priority danger sign under FHR monitoring during labour, requiring immediate urgent management and hospital referral.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section D14: 'Cord seen at vulva = PROLAPSED CORD — Manage urgently.' This is an obstetric emergency. The protocol flags this as a priority danger sign under FHR monitoring during labour, requiring immediate urgent management and hospital referral.

questions.patient_prompt: Durga has been in labour for 6 hours. She suddenly develops convulsions. After the convulsion ends, she is unconscious. Her diastolic BP measured before the episode was 115 mmHg.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct IMMEDIATE management sequence?

options.text: Give diazepam first, then check BP, then refer
Protect from injury, manage airway, insert IV slowly, give magnesium sulphate, give antihypertensive (diastolic >110), refer urgently
Give oxytocin to complete delivery first, then manage convulsions
Give glucose IV, antibiotics, and refer without magnesium sulphate

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B5 — Convulsions/Unconscious: Protocol sequence: 'Protect from fall and injury. Get help. Manage airway. After convulsion ends, help onto left side. Insert IV and give fluids slowly. Give magnesium sulphate. If diastolic BP >110 mmHg, give antihypertensive. Refer urgently.' This presentation = Eclampsia.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B5 — Convulsions/Unconscious: Protocol sequence: 'Protect from fall and injury. Get help. Manage airway. After convulsion ends, help onto left side. Insert IV and give fluids slowly. Give magnesium sulphate. If diastolic BP >110 mmHg, give antihypertensive. Refer urgently.' This presentation = Eclampsia.

questions.patient_prompt: Jaya is in labour. You gave magnesium sulphate loading dose for suspected eclampsia. After 4 hours, you are about to give the maintenance dose (5g IM). You check: knee jerk is present, urine output is 120 ml/4 hours, respiratory rate is 14 breaths/min.
questions.mcq_question: Should you give the next dose of magnesium sulphate?

options.text: Yes — all signs are within normal range
Yes — only urine output and knee jerk matter, not respiratory rate
No — respiratory rate is below 16/min, which is a contraindication
Yes — give a reduced half dose since respiratory rate is slightly low

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B13: 'Before giving the next dose of magnesium sulphate, ensure: knee jerk is present ✓, urine output >100 ml/4 hrs ✓, respiratory rate >16/min ✗.' Even ONE contraindication sign means DO NOT give the next dose. Respiratory rate of 14/min alone is sufficient to withhold. Antidote if toxicity: calcium gluconate 1g IV.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B13: 'Before giving the next dose of magnesium sulphate, ensure: knee jerk is present ✓, urine output >100 ml/4 hrs ✓, respiratory rate >16/min ✗.' Even ONE contraindication sign means DO NOT give the next dose. Respiratory rate of 14/min alone is sufficient to withhold. Antidote if toxicity: calcium gluconate 1g IV.

questions.patient_prompt: Rekha is 38 weeks pregnant and in early labour. Temperature is 38.4°C. Membranes ruptured approximately 20 hours ago. FHR is 168 bpm (sustained). She has foul-smelling vaginal discharge and lower abdominal tenderness.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct classification and action?

options.text: Risk of Uterine Infection — encourage fluids, monitor temperature every 4 hours
Malaria — give artesunate IM and refer
Uterine and Fetal Infection — insert IV, give IV/IM antibiotics, give oxytocin 10 IU IM after delivery, refer urgently
Upper Respiratory Tract Infection — advise safe soothing remedy and reassess

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section D21 + B15: Temperature ≥38°C with foul-smelling discharge/lower abdominal tenderness/FHR >160 bpm AND rupture of membranes >18 hours = Uterine and Fetal Infection. Actions: 'Insert IV, give fluids rapidly, give appropriate IM/IV antibiotics, give oxytocin 10 IU IM after delivery, refer urgently.'
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section D21 + B15: Temperature ≥38°C with foul-smelling discharge/lower abdominal tenderness/FHR >160 bpm AND rupture of membranes >18 hours = Uterine and Fetal Infection. Actions: 'Insert IV, give fluids rapidly, give appropriate IM/IV antibiotics, give oxytocin 10 IU IM after delivery, refer urgently.'

title: Newborn Care After Birth
short_description: Learn how to handle Newborn Care After Birth correctly based on WHO protocols.

questions.patient_prompt: A baby has just been delivered at a primary health post. The baby is breathing well and is in good condition. The mother is also stable.
questions.mcq_question: According to WHO protocol, when should the umbilical cord be clamped?

options.text: Immediately after birth to prevent blood loss
Only after the placenta is delivered
Not earlier than 1 minute after birth (delayed cord clamping)
After 10 minutes, once the cord stops pulsating completely

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! WHO Recommendation — Delayed Cord Clamping (Strong recommendation, moderate-quality evidence): 'Delayed umbilical cord clamping — not earlier than 1 minute after birth — is recommended for improved maternal and infant health and nutrition outcomes.'
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. WHO Recommendation — Delayed Cord Clamping (Strong recommendation, moderate-quality evidence): 'Delayed umbilical cord clamping — not earlier than 1 minute after birth — is recommended for improved maternal and infant health and nutrition outcomes.'

questions.patient_prompt: Baby born 10 minutes ago is in skin-to-skin contact with the mother. You notice the baby has not yet been bathed. A family member insists you bathe the baby immediately as per their custom.
questions.mcq_question: What is the WHO-recommended response?

options.text: Bathe the baby immediately to respect cultural customs — it is harmless
Bathe after 1 hour once the baby is stable
Delay bathing until 24 hours after birth; if cultural reasons require earlier, delay at least 6 hours
Bathe immediately only if the baby has blood or meconium on skin

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! WHO Postnatal Care Recommendation: 'Bathing should be delayed until 24 hours after birth. If this is not possible due to cultural reasons, bathing should be delayed for at least six hours.' Early bathing risks hypothermia.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. WHO Postnatal Care Recommendation: 'Bathing should be delayed until 24 hours after birth. If this is not possible due to cultural reasons, bathing should be delayed for at least six hours.' Early bathing risks hypothermia.

questions.patient_prompt: A newborn is delivered at a health facility. The baby is breathing on its own and is alert. The mother is stable. It has been 5 minutes since birth.
questions.mcq_question: According to protocol, what should you do regarding feeding AND eye care within the first hour?

options.text: Give sugar water to the baby until breastfeeding is established
Apply eye ointment only after bathing the baby
Apply tetracycline eye ointment to both eyes once; encourage breastfeeding when baby shows readiness — do NOT give pre-lacteal feeds
Wait 2 hours before initiating breastfeeding to allow the mother to rest

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section D19 — First Hour Care: 'Apply antiseptic eye drops or ointment (e.g. tetracycline) to both eyes once.' AND 'Encourage the mother to initiate breastfeeding when baby shows signs of readiness. DO NOT give artificial teats or pre-lacteal feeds — no water, sugar water, or local feeds.'
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section D19 — First Hour Care: 'Apply antiseptic eye drops or ointment (e.g. tetracycline) to both eyes once.' AND 'Encourage the mother to initiate breastfeeding when baby shows signs of readiness. DO NOT give artificial teats or pre-lacteal feeds — no water, sugar water, or local feeds.'

questions.patient_prompt: A baby is born through clear amniotic fluid and starts breathing spontaneously after gentle stimulation (rubbing the back). An assistant asks whether to suction the baby's mouth and nose as routine procedure.
questions.mcq_question: What is the WHO-recommended response?

options.text: Yes — always suction to clear the airway and prevent aspiration
Suction only the nose, not the mouth
Do NOT perform routine suctioning — it is not recommended for babies born through clear fluid who breathe on their own
Suction only if the baby cries weakly in the first minute

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! WHO Newborn Resuscitation Recommendation (Strong recommendation, high-quality evidence): 'In neonates born through clear amniotic fluid who start breathing on their own after birth, suctioning of the mouth and nose should NOT be performed.' Routine suction is harmful and not recommended.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. WHO Newborn Resuscitation Recommendation (Strong recommendation, high-quality evidence): 'In neonates born through clear amniotic fluid who start breathing on their own after birth, suctioning of the mouth and nose should NOT be performed.' Routine suction is harmful and not recommended.

questions.patient_prompt: Baby Aryan is born at a health post. His mother is HIV-positive and on ART. The delivery was uncomplicated. The baby is breathing well. You are deciding whether to delay cord clamping given the mother's HIV status.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct action according to WHO?

options.text: Clamp immediately — delayed clamping increases HIV transmission risk to the baby
Consult the doctor before clamping — no protocol applies here
Proceed with delayed cord clamping (≥1 min) — there is no evidence it increases HIV transmission risk
Clamp after 30 seconds as a compromise between benefits and HIV risk

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! WHO Delayed Cord Clamping Recommendation: 'Late cord clamping is recommended even among women living with HIV or women with unknown HIV status. There is no evidence that delaying cord clamping increases the possibility of HIV transmission from mother to newborn.' The proven benefits outweigh theoretical harms.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. WHO Delayed Cord Clamping Recommendation: 'Late cord clamping is recommended even among women living with HIV or women with unknown HIV status. There is no evidence that delaying cord clamping increases the possibility of HIV transmission from mother to newborn.' The proven benefits outweigh theoretical harms.

title: Postnatal Follow-up: Bleeding Management
short_description: Learn how to handle Postnatal Follow-up: Bleeding Management correctly based on WHO protocols.

questions.patient_prompt: Sarita delivered 2 days ago at home. She comes to the health post saying she is bleeding heavily — her pad soaks in under 5 minutes. The uterus feels soft.
questions.mcq_question: What is your FIRST action?

options.text: Massage the uterine fundus until it is hard, call for help, give oxytocin 10 IU IM
Give ergometrine 0.2 mg immediately
Ask her to rest and return if bleeding continues
Give her ORS to prevent dehydration from blood loss

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B4 + B10: For heavy postpartum bleeding with a soft uterus: 'Call for extra help. Massage uterus until it is hard and give oxytocin 10 IU IM.' Uterine massage is always the first physical action. Ergometrine is CONTRAINDICATED if pre-eclampsia, hypertension, or retained placenta is present.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B4 + B10: For heavy postpartum bleeding with a soft uterus: 'Call for extra help. Massage uterus until it is hard and give oxytocin 10 IU IM.' Uterine massage is always the first physical action. Ergometrine is CONTRAINDICATED if pre-eclampsia, hypertension, or retained placenta is present.

questions.patient_prompt: Leela is 3 days postpartum and presents with fever (39°C), foul-smelling vaginal discharge, and lower abdominal tenderness. She delivered vaginally at home.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct classification and treatment?

options.text: Normal postpartum lochia — advise hygiene and return in 2 days
UTI — give oral antibiotics and encourage fluids
Postpartum infection (Endometritis) — give appropriate IV/IM antibiotics, refer urgently if she becomes weak or develops worsening abdominal pain
Malaria — check for fever and give antimalarials

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B15 — Infection + D21: Fever ≥38°C with foul-smelling lochia and abdominal tenderness postpartum = Postpartum Endometritis. Protocol: Give antibiotics (Clindamycin + Gentamicin). 'If signs persist or mother becomes weak or has abdominal pain postpartum, refer urgently to hospital.'
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B15 — Infection + D21: Fever ≥38°C with foul-smelling lochia and abdominal tenderness postpartum = Postpartum Endometritis. Protocol: Give antibiotics (Clindamycin + Gentamicin). 'If signs persist or mother becomes weak or has abdominal pain postpartum, refer urgently to hospital.'

questions.patient_prompt: At a postnatal follow-up 1 hour after delivery, the mother has moderate vaginal bleeding. The uterus is hard and round. The pad is not soaked. Blood loss is estimated at 260 ml and has now stopped. You are at a primary health post.
questions.mcq_question: According to protocol, can she be discharged?

options.text: Yes — bleeding has stopped and uterus is well contracted
Yes — as long as you give her iron tablets and advise to return if bleeding restarts
No — she must stay at the facility for 24 hours with monitoring every 30 minutes for 4 hours
Yes — only if the blood loss was under 300 ml and uterus is firm

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B4 + D20: 'If blood loss ≥250 ml but bleeding has stopped: plan to keep the woman in the facility for 24 hours. Monitor intensively every 30 minutes for 4 hours — blood pressure, pulse, vaginal bleeding, uterus.' WHO states: 'DO NOT discharge before 24 hours' after birth.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B4 + D20: 'If blood loss ≥250 ml but bleeding has stopped: plan to keep the woman in the facility for 24 hours. Monitor intensively every 30 minutes for 4 hours — blood pressure, pulse, vaginal bleeding, uterus.' WHO states: 'DO NOT discharge before 24 hours' after birth.

questions.patient_prompt: Divya is 5 days postpartum. She felt dizzy and fainted at home. She has heavy vaginal bleeding. On examination, diastolic BP is 95 mmHg on two readings, and she has severe headache. Uterus is soft.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct multi-step action?

options.text: Give ergometrine for the uterine atony and refer for hypertension
Manage postpartum bleeding (massage, oxytocin, IV fluids); DO NOT give ergometrine due to elevated BP; refer urgently to hospital
Treat hypertension first with antihypertensives, then manage bleeding
Give misoprostol for bleeding, antihypertensive for BP, and discharge with follow-up

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Sections B4 + B10 + D22: Combined emergency — PPH with hypertension. Manage uterine atony with massage and oxytocin. Critical: 'DO NOT give ergometrine to women with eclampsia, pre-eclampsia or known hypertension.' Diastolic 95 mmHg on two readings = hypertension. Refer urgently to hospital.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Sections B4 + B10 + D22: Combined emergency — PPH with hypertension. Manage uterine atony with massage and oxytocin. Critical: 'DO NOT give ergometrine to women with eclampsia, pre-eclampsia or known hypertension.' Diastolic 95 mmHg on two readings = hypertension. Refer urgently to hospital.

questions.patient_prompt: Kavita is 6 hours postpartum. Heavy bleeding from a perineal tear persists despite pressure. On examination the tear extends through the anal sphincter into the rectal mucosa.
questions.mcq_question: What is the correct classification and action?

options.text: Second-degree tear — suture at the health post using local anaesthesia
Apply pressure for another 10 minutes and reassess
Third-degree tear — cover with clean pad, refer urgently to hospital. Do NOT attempt suturing at primary level
Apply antiseptic and give oral antibiotics; suture only if bleeding doesn't stop in 30 minutes

questions.explanation_correct: Correct! Section B12 + D22: A tear involving the anal sphincter and rectal mucosa = Third/Fourth-degree tear. Protocol: 'Cover it with a clean pad and refer the woman urgently to hospital.' Suturing at primary level is explicitly NOT recommended for this degree of tear.
questions.explanation_wrong: Incorrect. Section B12 + D22: A tear involving the anal sphincter and rectal mucosa = Third/Fourth-degree tear. Protocol: 'Cover it with a clean pad and refer the woman urgently to hospital.' Suturing at primary level is explicitly NOT recommended for this degree of tear.
